speaker: interviewer
text: I will ask you to stand up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just getting comfortable. [LAUGHS] Toward the end, when we were out of this block, we literally went from one night to the next. We had heard the rumors about Count Bernadotte. Didn't quite know who or what, but as it turned our later on, that wonderful man had long, long before that tried to make a deal with Himmler to exchange against prisoners, German prisoners, you know, Swedish, and Danish and so on. And when the war went well for Germany, didn't want any part of it. Toward the end, Himmler agreed. And it was agreed that they would come and take us, which was the last concentration camp being hid. And also, I must say that in the concentration camp, after the bunker, I accidentally met Gemma Gluck, a sweet old lady. Well, she wasn't old then, I just thought she was. Sweet sister of former mayor of New York, La Guardia, who was married to the Hungarian police president. And this is how she came, because I said, what does an American do in Ravensbruck or in a camp, look exactly like La Guardia. Like chocolate pudding. Sweet woman. And I didn't know really. It didn't mean much to me, except I thought, I don't know where my mother is. Perhaps there's a young girl helping her. And I helped her a lot. And Gemma Gluck also suddenly disappeared. In other words, they also needed her as a bargaining chip, of course, for some-- some big German who knows what. They-- I don't know why only French, Belgian, and-- but I know that the clique of the goal was in that group. And somehow, I managed to get into this group of the 700 women who were supposed to be brought out by Count Bernadotte and managed to get my friend Eva, a Czech, also through by putting a scarf around her and teaching her the Dutch pronunciation of where we lived in Amsterdam, and told her not to open her mouth, because there was one less who could go from the Belgian and Dutch people. And they were supposed to come for us, and they didn't come. We were brought up to a little camp I didn't even know was there. A man's camp above, perhaps 1/2 a mile from our camp. It was already completely empty. They already had gone. I don't know, evacuated, freed, whatever. And there only were a couple of Czech guys, because there were sick people there. Shortly before I got my [INAUDIBLE] for the very first time, American Red Cross packages were allowed in the camp. And that is when I was officially declared non-Jewish, which means I didn't get a package, either. No package. No Red Cross package. And up there in that camp, there were a few sick people, because they had gotten packages, and unfortunately opened it and ate butter like this and chocolate like this. And got of course, dysentery and very sick. So these two Czech guys was there to guard them, invited Eva and me for the first night. They had stolen a lot of, I don't know, whiskey or some drinks which they had grabbed from the Germans who had fled. For a drink. That very first night, while we were there, Eva was drunk. I drank that thing, it didn't do anything to me. It was like water. I have no idea why. Having a very good time, control came. And these two guys, one of them lifted me up and took me into one of the rooms. It turned out to be the morgue. I was spending my first night so-called free in the morgue, where those people who had dysentery had died. Man, I was on the same slab with a dead man, in the same sheet, staying there all night long until these Germans for control, which never came for control, only to drink. They heard that there was whiskey to be had, so they came up to drink, disappeared. And Eva was in another room, also in the morgue, but she slept through the whole thing, because she was drunk anyway. The next day, they marched us down, and the Swedish people didn't come. And they didn't come, and they didn't come. There was-- this was not-- the war had not ended. It was about two weeks before the end of the war. There was bombing, there was shooting, the Nazis were still marching around. We were outside the camp, and we had decided, all of us, we're not going to back in there again. Whatever happens, they must come eventually. Much, much later, about two days and two nights later, they came. 10 very, very tired men on trucks. On old, broken down trucks. The Swedish leader with his assistant, and so on. And the first time I saw and heard English-- I mean, I've heard English before. But I saw an American was outside the concentration camp. The drivers were American boys. They had fought in Africa, had been taken prisoners, were exchanged by the Swedish Red Cross against German prisoners, and had volunteered for the Swedish Red Cross to drive and get us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they came to us, they were already 48 hours on the way. This is why they were so late, because the bridges were already blown up. It was the end of the war, and everything was very hard to get through. They came to us, and heard-- I saw the American cap, and I heard somebody say, does anybody speak English here? And there he was, an American. Can you imagine it? Outside of a German concentration camp, the war still going. It was incredible. They put us on the cars. We were supposed to go first to Lubeck, which was open city. In other words, on the Geneva Conference, not to be bombed. From there to Denmark, and then to Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us that it was very hard to get to us, so we had to leave immediately. We had some of the wounded there, who were sitting in front. Now if-- whenever there is a Red Cross transport, what is supposed to be done, the commander goes-- commandant down goes to the radio and says, attention, attention now. 700 women are leaving Ravensbruck. They will go. They will travel this and this route. And this route is not supposed to be bombed or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started travelling, and within five minutes, there were German tanks and artillery mixing between our Red Cross cars, which had plenty of Red Crosses. They had more than we, trying to assure a safe retreat. So they stopped. They said, we have to lose them. We cannot go with them. They're going to be bombed if they stay with us. So we went into a forest, the Swedish leader, and we spent the entire night in that forest. And while the Nazis or Nazi soldiers, I don't know, were walking still around us, guarding us. And the machine guns were going, and the bombs were flying, this American next to me talked to me about America.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me what it was like. That nobody needed papers there. Everybody was driving a car. And that you could go anywhere you wanted to go. It was very strange in this German forest, with the lights of the-- you know, bombs still, your hear that. And the Germans marching around. Hearing an American talk about America, that was really free. No papers necessary. Nothing. And I remember that for two years since that incident, in Mechelen, with the powder compact-- I hadn't looked in a mirror, nor had I ever thought of looking in a mirror. And suddenly, I didn't want sleep or-- or food, or-- or warmth. I just wanted to be beautiful. Look good, not like a wretched thing. And then I realized it didn't matter to him at all. He didn't understand, but he somehow knew we had gone through plenty. The next day, we went back on the road and travelling . And you know, we were the happiest convoy that ever travelled through a war. We were singing, we were laughing, and then it happened. We drove right through the last-- the last hold the Germans held. At the same instance, British flyers, from 50 meters, dropped bombs. We were not supposed to go that way. We were supposed to go another route, but because of those tanks which came between us, we-- the leader had gone another route, right through it. And then there was an inferno. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything flew. Fire, trucks upside down, arms, legs, heads, all over the place. Screams. It was awful. I landed in a sand hole. I assumed I was dead, because I didn't feel any pain. I felt nothing. I know that John, Johnny, Johnny, was his name. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yes. He told me that one of his friends had lost an eye, another one was wounded. But nobody knew what was going on. He just told me we had to run into the forest. And I  know that I was walking very slowly, totally like suspended in air. I didn't feel any pain. I was sure I was dead, just all because I didn't worry about life. I didn't worry at all. And I had no pain. But then I sat down under a tree, and I was wondering where my other shoe was. I missed a shoe. I had only one shoe. Two sizes too large, I know, one of those mountain shoes, but only one. And I saw the pus of this wound running down my leg, and I wondered why it didn't hurt. And reality did not come back to me very soon. It took a while. And then I saw the leaves turning in the forest. You know, the bushes, the branches, swinging. And it was soldiers camouflaged. A whole German colony of people, military, was there with their guns up to the heaven. The branches, you know, to camouflage their things and their uniforms, all leaves and so on. And I was in a shooting gallery, right here. All over the place. And that's when I came back to reality, thinking, it can't be. That's not possible. I couldn't die here, right here. They paid no attention to me. They were like this. But it was surreal to sit there, as we are sitting here, that close. And then very slowly, I crawled on my hands and my knees and my belly, out. And the road was a disaster. We had lost so many people, so many wounded. And the Germans who wanted to leave had left their little villages to get retreat. They came over our dead. They crawled over our dead into our cars, which were turned over, and stole the Red Cross packages which still were there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't have enough trucks anymore. There was no doctor, not even among the Germans. The generals kept saluting, saying how sorry they were, and it was awful. They tried to help as much to turn the trucks over. We knew they would come back to finish the British [INAUDIBLE] not us, but them. And we had to get out of there. While there were only less than half of our cars left, we brought whatever we could into the trucks. We had to leave the wounded behind. Of course the dead, but the wounded too. And then this was 20 kilometers to Lubeck, which is about, I don't know, 50 miles. Something like that. And you know, in Europe, you have-- every mile, you have a little stone where it says so and so many kilometers, so and so many kilometers. These 20 kilometers were the longest in the world. It was a country road. It was the end of April. Hot for this time of year. Unbelievably hot. And it was, you know, one of those dusty country roads with ditches on both sides. And the truck was overloaded. And it went like a snail. Much too slow. And I kept counting the kilometers. And it was bad. My lips were burnt, my tongue was on fire. This was awful. And I kept hanging on, and Eva too, because we had many, much too many people in that one truck. And they were pushing out because there was-- it was covered, and they couldn't see, and they couldn't feel. And they were frightened. And the second time we heard-- and Johnny had told us to tell the others. I should tell the others, the moment we hear planes again, to jump out and just run into the next field. And the moment planes came again, which was more than a few minutes later, I didn't have to jump. They all pushed me out. And I know I ran into a German potato field, and Johnny on top of me, was like a cross. And they came back to the same spot. I don't know. I don't know how many were shot down, how many were killed again. I was lying there for a long time. When we went back into the trucks, half of the women didn't want to go back anymore. They had enough. You know, they were so frightened from the closed-- being closed in. We left again. I was hanging on for dear life. And this street was like a choreographed scene. Death on both sides of the road. Turned over tanks, turned over trucks, soldiers pinned in their trucks. Horses with their mouths wide open. I mean, laughing. It was insanity. It was crazy. This whole road, nothing but death. And as we drove on, I saw-- we passed a man. An old man in the middle of the road, with a long beard, and a sign, and a cane, blind. And he was walking along, trying to get away from all of this. And for a second there, I wanted to say, let's stop. Let's take him with us. And then all of this, this hatred came up in me. And I said, this goddamn German, die. I want you to die. And I never said stop. And we drove on. And I think there were six or seven kilometers left. I was on fire. We came-- I don't know. I couldn't count anymore, nothing. We came to Lubeck. We made it to Lubeck. And there was Red Cross there. And there was barrels of soup. And somebody caught me. And then-- I had lost the other shoe in the soup. And I know we were supposed to stay there that night, but we didn't. The order had come, we can't stay there anymore. Either they expected to be bombed, who was bombing, we didn't know. In other words, I had no time. Because Johnny said to me, don't. Don't, don't, don't go off to Sweden. Stay. And then come with me to America right away. Go to America. Don't go back to Sweden or go back to Amsterdam, anything. And I know that I couldn't walk anymore. I was on fire. My mouth was on fire, and my leg was on fire. He carried me for at least eight kilometers, all through the town, into a forest. Into a forest-- Danish forest, where Danish cars were. He had put me on a seat. And he took his dog tag off, not the metal one, the plastic one. And he gave it to me. And he said, that's who I am. Don't forget, that's who I am. I had given him previous my address in Amsterdam. And that's all I know. I woke up in a hospital in Denmark, in Copenhagen. Didn't know where I was, either. I had no recollection of that until much, much later. What happened is that when we came to Denmark, the flags were on half mast, because they already knew about what happened to our transport. And what I heard also later is that my twin sister, who had been living in London, just by turning on the news, BBC, they announced a list of survivors. And my name was on it. And this is how she heard for the first time in two years, first of all, that I was alive, and where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they knew it also, I guess, from radio. I don't know, because I was out of it. I was really out of it. And then when we came to Sweden, I don't even know how long I was in the hospital in Copenhagen. I can't tell you. I really don't know. Not long. I had no speech, either. No recollection, and no speech. It came back later on. The Swedish king also greeted us with-- when we arrived. And I found out later only 120 of us made it out of the 700.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before that later on, they asked you to testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, they came also to New York. I was already in New York. I testified in New York against that bastard Harry. The one who-- you know. Harry is the one who took the 2,000 guilders and so on. And very interesting. In Switzerland, the British officers-- it's-- it's really ridiculous if you think of it. We couldn't-- we tried. They were there for days and days and days. They were still working under old British law. And old British law said that you must have an eyewitness. In other words, I must have seen somebody being shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't. I mean, I know that from the thousands of people who went to Ravensbruck, many less came out. And they didn't die because the food too good, because they over-ate or under-worked, right? There were an awful lot of people not making it. Heavy factory work, kicking, and you know, the normal-- normal. The things which happened in camps. And yet, because they could not find anyone who saw-- they saw to it. Why do you think what happened in Auschwitz? It was the prisoners who did the work, right? Voluntary or involuntary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't think that it wasn't thought out by the Germans. It was very carefully thought out. Very carefully thought out. They knew exactly judgment day will come one day. Let's see how they judge us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't find anyone in Ravensbruck. Kicked? Yes. Being beaten half to death? Yes. But actually in front of it? That wasn't done in front of it. After you were liberated, where did you go? After you were [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I was for a long time in Sweden. I stayed in Sweden for a long time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should've stayed there. First of all, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the transition. It's like you are born again. Really born again, and you start from new. As far as I'm concerned, I wanted the two years back they stole from me. And I ran a lot. I mean, literally ran. Physically ran. I couldn't sit still. I wanted to be in a park, I wanted to be in a museum. I wanted to be here, I wanted to be everywhere. I wanted to make up the time. It took me forever to know that I couldn't. But meanwhile, I started running. I hated everyone. I hated the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Didn't trust anyone. And there was a lot of pain in me, which I didn't allow to come out. I just wanted to breathe twice as fast, so I'd get one more in. Make up time. This was my-- my biggest thing. The other thing was that I knew that if I would go home, or when I went home, it wouldn't work, because I was not the one who had left. And even so, with all the love and compassion and understanding, they would look for me, I no longer existed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And therefore, I have always said, you know, Thomas Wolfe, you can't go home again? Yes, you can. The question one must ask is should one? I should not have. I can't talk about other people. I don't know. I should not have gone home. Because they were looking for me, and I wasn't there anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even so, with-- you know, they couldn't do right for me anyway. I was on a different planet altogether. Totally different planet. I wouldn't hear what they said. I was with my dead friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember hours and hours I would walk, looking at the stones, thinking, is it true that there are worms underneath? Would they be where my friends are? What is underneath there? Or are there little flowers, violets, or pretty flowers for them? I could not-- not that I couldn't relate to life, but I figured, who are these people? I was sitting in a room full of people, and they would talk to me. I wasn't there. I was sitting there, but I wasn't there at all. Not at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: It became so bad, that I couldn't walk one day. Nothing wrong with me physically, nothing. I mean, the doctor said that I was terribly anemic and needed 100,000 of those horse shots. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we came to Sweden, you see, we were-- first of all, again in quarantine. We were fed by the spoon that was weighed ounce by ounce by ounce, so that our stomach could get used, which was very wise. But the pain was not taken care of. And I feel-- I felt it then, feel it now, the war has not ended. I never had the chance to kill one of those bastards. And it will not end for me until I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they-- they've never given me a chance to fight back, to fight anybody. I mean, you know, submit. Submission. And then what? Free. What is it, free? What do you mean, free? How am I free? I know there are Nazis sitting in big, big jobs in Germany today. I know they live happily ever after in South America with the help of the pope and with the help of Americans. I know that Nazis came into this country, because all America was concerned about is the communists. God forbid the communists. So they let the Nazis in to tell them about the communists. So how do you think it makes us feel? It makes me feel? It's not over. I knew it wasn't over. Why? It's a contagious disease that spread. Nobody has ever stopped the foul, malignant seeds from spreading. Nobody. It goes on. You have the heirs of the Nazis and the heirs of their Nazis. Where did you go after Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Amsterdam, which I shouldn't have done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was there since I got out, and my sister was there. And her husband was there. And my fiance was there. But I wasn't there. I was, but I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how was that first time--

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, let us say you invited me for lunch, 1:00. My in-laws. I wouldn't come at 1:00. You're never going to tell me again I have to do such and such and such and such a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want me to stand up? I will not. I will sit. You want me at 1:00? I come at 3:00. Perhaps. Perhaps I don't come. No orders. Even though they were not orders. Of course they weren't orders. But somebody wanted me to do something at a certain time, and I don't want to do this at a certain time unless I want to. You still feel that way now, today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course not. Of course not. Because I know the difference. But it took a long, long time. It also took a long time to realize I cannot make up the time that was stolen from me. Because it's the only thing you can't replace.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can replace everything else. Not time. And it's the most precious thing there is. And that is so what? I mean, you know. They-- they take my career, anyway. They screwed up my career but royally. And who got punished for it? Who did really get punished for it? The little guys. Not the big ones. The little guys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it has not ended, and it is not over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know when it ever will be, but it isn't over. You realize, this is, I think-- I may be wrong-- but I think this is just about the first time in history that there weren't civilians. I mean, what did we have to do with the war? Even with soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no weapons to fight. We had nothing to give back what they did to us. Not even that much. It's not a matter of revenge, it's a matter of justice.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are many, many-- it's not only one thing. First of all, I firmly believe then as now, my time wasn't up. I'm a fatalist. I've always been one, just as a bit more pronounced, let's say. I believe that each and every one of us, from the moment we are born, has their cards dealt where they're going to end up. Now, you can go left, and you can go right. You have a choice there. But you're going to end up where you're meant to end up. I have no doubt about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is I'm a fatalist, my time wasn't up. Also, I didn't want to die. I wanted to live so badly. So badly. No matter the-- the price. I can't say I would've sold my soul for it, but everything else. And I could not conceive of that I was born to die and rot in the hell of Auschwitz. How could this be? Why? Why? And luck. Let's face it. You can want to live as much as you want and be as strong as you can be. If you don't have any luck, I mean, I wasn't the second or the fourth when they choose for their Russian Roulette. I could've been. I had no idea where to stand. So it is a combination of many things. Positive thinking helps, to be able to go outside yourself and do incredible odds physically, as mentally, as emotionally. Incredible odds.
